did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) for the classification of melanoma cancer stages (Stage 1 and Stage 2) using the ISIC 2019 dataset. The study achieved 95.72% accuracy and 99.72% confidence in detecting skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) on the ISIC 2019 dataset to detect melanoma stages. It details a specific methodology including data preprocessing, model training (30 epochs), and evaluation, reporting new results such as 95.72% accuracy and 99.72% confidence. Although published in a conference (ICCUBEA), it contains original algorithmic development and validation rather than merely summarizing prior literature.

title: Machine Learning Approach in Melanoma Cancer Stage Detection
journal: 2023 7th International Conference On Computing, Communication, Control And Automation (ICCUBEA)
brief_summary: This study proposes a machine learning approach to detect Stage 1 and Stage 2 melanoma cancer using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN). The researchers utilized the ISIC 2019 dataset, which contains 25,331 images of skin lesions, splitting it into a 70% training set and a 30% validation set. The CNN model was trained for 30 epochs using augmented data to classify melanoma stages. The primary evaluation metric reported was the model's accuracy and confidence level on the test dataset. The study achieved a final accuracy of 95.72% and a confidence of 99.72% in detecting the specified melanoma stages. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of CNNs in automating early melanoma detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on detecting Stage 1 and Stage 2 of melanoma cancer. The dataset contains melanoma lesions in about 30.3% of the images. Specific counts mentioned: 2938 melanomas >1.5 mm, 5471 melanomas between 0.76 and 1.5 mm, and 16921 melanomas <0.76 mm. The test set included 1800 images of Stage 1 and 1499 images of Stage 2.
The remaining images in the ISIC 2019 dataset (approximately 69.7%) were classified as benign nevi. These served as the non-melanoma class for the binary classification task.

all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is to detect 'Stage 1 and Stage 2 of Melanoma cancer' using a 'stage 1 classification system and a stage 2 classification system'. The model uses a CNN with a sigmoid/softmax layer to output probabilities for discrete diagnostic categories (Stage 1 or Stage 2). The dataset is described as having 'binary and multiclass datasets' for melanoma, and the results report accuracy (95.72%) and confidence (99.72%) for classifying images into these specific stages.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: A compilation of skin disease datasets made available for the ISBI challenge in 2019, containing images of melanoma lesions and benign nevi.

use.training: 70% of the dataset (17,731 images) was used for training the CNN model using augmented data.
use.validation: 30% of the dataset (7,600 images) was used as validation data to improve accuracy.
use.testing: The model was tested using 3,299 images (30% of the dataset), which included 1,800 images of Stage 1 and 1,499 images of Stage 2 melanoma.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It reported model performance metrics such as accuracy (95.72%) and confidence (99.72%) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2019.notes: The model was tested using 3,299 images (30% of the dataset). The test set consisted of 1,800 images of Stage 1 melanoma and 1,499 images of Stage 2 melanoma. The model was trained for 30 epochs with a batch size of 32.

architecture_details.description: The CNN architecture consists of a convolutional layer, a pooling layer, a normalization layer, an activation function, and a sigmoid/softmax layer. The input images are converted to grayscale and processed through convolutional filters to extract features, followed by max pooling to reduce spatial resolution. The feature maps are sent to fully connected layers, and the output is converted into a probability distribution using the softmax function for classification.

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected layer with Softmax activation

preprocessing_techniques: Conversion of colored 3D (RGB) images to grayscale (1D pixel values) to reduce computational complexity
Application of a median filter to reduce noise from skin images after grayscale conversion
Data augmentation (used for training the model)
Splitting the dataset into training (70%) and validation/testing (30%) sets

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The paper states that 'augmented data' was used to train the CNN model. However, it does not specify the particular geometric transforms, color transforms, or synthetic generation methods employed, nor does it provide probabilities or ranges for these augmentations.

conclusions: The study concludes that Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) are an effective approach for detecting Stage 1 and Stage 2 melanoma cancer, achieving a classification accuracy of 95.72% and a confidence level of 99.72%.

The proposed CNN-based system offers a timely and automated detection strategy for skin diseases, addressing the limitations of existing manual and semi-automated approaches which require significant computer resources, time, and exhibit lower accuracy.

The implementation of the model on the ISIC 2019 dataset demonstrates the potential for deploying such machine learning systems in clinical applications, including skin cancer diagnostic labs, hospitals, and health monitoring devices.

The study highlights that early detection of melanoma is crucial due to its rapid spread to lymph nodes and high mortality rates, and that machine learning techniques can significantly aid in this task by learning patterns from clinical data.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC 2019 dataset, which may introduce population or dataset bias, and lacks external validation on independent clinical cohorts.
The paper does not report standard statistical metrics such as sensitivity, specificity, precision, recall, F1-score, or AUC-ROC, relying only on accuracy (95.72%) and confidence (99.72%).
No formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals for the performance metrics are provided, limiting the statistical robustness of the claims.
The specific data augmentation techniques employed are not detailed, raising potential concerns about reproducibility and the extent of data leakage prevention.

The CNN architecture is described as having only four layers (input, convolutional, pooling, normalization, fully connected), which is unusually shallow for modern deep learning tasks and may limit feature extraction capability.
The paper mentions using an 'updated whale optimization technique' in the introduction but does not clearly integrate or report results for this optimizer in the methodology or results sections.
The claim of '99.72% confidence' is vague and not a standard machine learning evaluation metric; it is unclear if this refers to prediction confidence scores or a statistical confidence interval.

The dataset split is described inconsistently: Section IV.A states a 70/30 split for training/validation, while Section V states the test set comprises 30% of the data, creating ambiguity about the true hold-out test set size and independence.
The study focuses only on Stage 1 and Stage 2 melanoma, limiting the generalizability of the model to later stages of the disease or other skin lesion types beyond benign nevi.
As a conference paper with limited space, the methodology lacks sufficient detail on hyperparameter tuning, loss functions, and optimizer choices, hindering full reproducibility.
